rationale: The citing paper repeatedly uses Wansink and Chandon (2006) as the key prior evidence that low-fat claims can increase actual intake, including in the introduction, conceptual background, and discussion. It positions its central research question as testing whether that low-fat overconsumption effect generalizes to other health-claim types and why, and it explicitly reports replication-consistent low-fat results in its own studies. This is not a passing mention; the cited study materially anchors the paper's framing, hypotheses, and contribution claim.
relation_description: The citing paper builds directly on Wansink and Chandon (2006) by taking their low-fat overconsumption finding as a baseline and extending it to a broader theory that distinguishes hedonic versus functional health claims. It adds new mechanism evidence (goal accessibility/conflict) and boundary conditions showing when increased consumption can be reduced.
